body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

transcript.text: I see a lot of people coming up to the door here, taking one glance inside, thinking better of it. Uh yeah, we don't have a large pool of candidates for Minute Taker here at the moment. Yeah. Who needs minutes anymore? I mean, we've got a full audio transcript of this like whisper, you know.ai would get us a perfectly serviceable transcript with only a mod of hallucinations in which, you know, we pledge to do a variety of things that we probably don't want to do. >> Yeah. I mean, the summarization is frankly better than the voice track in my experience working with these I work with these audio tools a lot and like it's it's kind of amazing what um what the hallucinations here into various statements. All right, we are we are getting close to commencing this meeting as it is now 9:31. Um however, one of the key people has wandered out of the room momentarily and we are hoping that he will return and when he does at that time commence. I'm also hoping to see some people logged in remotely um including a gentleman by the name of Elliot Leer. It' be good to have him here. >> Yeah, I I must you know he must be concerned about the traffic driving down here and decided to stay home. I drove here actually from France so not not that far. Um, >> well, I mean, he's he's what? He's Switzerland. It is. >> Yeah. Yeah. So, it's it's not that much farther. >> I hadn't done the northern route of the Pyrenees before. Um, you know, when you go to Beeritz and then around to San Sebastian and then like take that and so it's it's beautiful, beautiful country there. So, Okay, we really do need Lars back. So, we will be starting momentarily. I promise. I mean, I guess I could just do administr we can play our game of can we find a noteaker. All right. So, welcome welcome everyone on behalf of uh Lisa who you see up on the screen and myself to this ITF123 meeting of the moderation procedures brick and group mod pod. Thanks for being here in the room here. The few the devout are present. This is uh not exactly throngs here but who knows you know we could have late comers uh appear at any moment. Up on the screen here is something uh that's called a note well. uh you may have seen this if you have attended any other ITF activity this week or indeed at any ITF in uh the past as far as I can remember. Uh but please do understand and abide by the various things that one should note well and note really well as this slide here suggests. Um, if you're unfamiliar with just kind of the basics of working through this, if you're seeing these slides somehow and are not logged into the MedCo tool, um, not sure how many people that would be the case for, uh, these are some tips for working with meo and various resources that can support you in that endeavor. We have a very simple agenda today, uh, which is good. We making incredible amount of progress on the list. Uh, I think Lisa and I have both been really heartened to see the direction that these conversations have taken. Uh, but before we dig into it, um, anybody here feel like taking notes for this meeting? Of the very few people we have in this room, it would be great to have a notetaker. Um, in parallel, I'll ask if anyone would like to bash the agenda or talk about any documents other than the one that we plan to dig into here today. You can take a few notes. Thanks so much. Notes are being taken. I still do not see Mr. Leer in the list. Am I dismissing him here? Um, okay. Well, hopefully he'll be able to join in because he has been doing a lot of the heavy lifting, a lot of the work resolving those PRs in GitHub, and he deserves to be here to see the moment of glory when we all agree right here. If we can go for this all. Yes. Hey, >> there are six. There are six. >> Yes. Yes. >> Okay. Well, uh you know, with that, I I I think our plan here as we see on the agenda is to talk about this for a bit. Let's take 15 minutes at the end and ask ourselves if we think we're on the right track here and if uh maybe something like a working group last call could be in our immediate future. But with that, um, Lars, would you like to come up and I will somehow figure out how to switch these slides out. Good morning. Um, Elliot is, I guess, remotely participating. >> I I'm not I don't I don't >> He's not there yet. Okay. Well, if somebody sees him appear, let me know and I will tell him he should flick his camera on so that he can raise his hand when he wants to say something so I know to stop babbling. >> Get slides up here. Sure slides. >> Yeah, those are these are the most boring slides ever, which is a good good sign in this particular case. Oh, I have control even. Excellent. Right. Good morning. So, I'm Lars Egert. Elliot Leer is not yet remotely available, but he's my co-editor for this working group um document that we believe is um finishing uh or at least in our opinion. Should something happen when I click it >> should >> it doesn't. >> Um okay, I can try to advance manually. >> All right, thank you. Uh so the TLDDR here is uh we did a bunch of revisions um between uh Bangkok and now um basically whenever we thought that we had a stable thing for the working group to look at we pushed one. I'm going to briefly go through the changes that were made during that time. Um we have zero open PRs at the moment. Um and we also have uh zero content related issues. we have like two tracking/tooling issues that we may or may not uh need to do anything about depending on whether we're ready for work last call or not. So as far as the editors know we are done. Um this is the optimistic statement here and the question at the end will be uh John already said this we believe as editors we might be ready for working group as call but what do you believe next? So next few slides it's >> not working by the way. >> Okay excellent. So this just goes very briefly through what we changed, right? Um so in in ' 04 uh we just basically added there was a discussion whether giving some examples of what uh disruptive behavior looks like would be useful. I was personally a bit skeptical because we had that in the uh charter for the IGF discussion list and it only caused people to uh argue that what they did wasn't explicitly listed in the examples and therefore it wasn't covered and it was clear examples. Anyway, we decided we put it into an appendix that's non-normative and so the people who find it helpful have it and by shoving it at the end and labeling it very aggressively as non-normative maybe it alleviates the confusion. uh among those that think that only things that are on this list are disruptive. Um in 04 uh we dropped BCP14 language so that the document no longer uses capital uh verbs. Um and um we also obviously stopped the references. Um that doesn't mean that what this document says isn't normative. It just means that um the consensus of the working group was that PCP14 language in process documents is maybe sort of confusing and we shouldn't use it and so we don't. >> So so just real quick uh is anybody watching this in the video have we lost all the video because my display suggests you don't have a speaker camera anymore. Speaker camera working for people who are there on the uh Okay, good. Yeah, it's just lost on my display. >> You can just look this way. >> Um did Elliot show up in the meantime by the way? >> I've not seen him. Okay. >> Yeah. >> Uh right. Um in '05 um so there there was a long-standing issue um or a long-standing discussion about how the moderator team intersects with moderation that the chairs are tasked to do already specifically the chairs of ITF working groups. Um and we did an attempt uh to clarify that. Um uh going to fast forward there's there's going to be a final attempt a few revisions later that actually fixes it. But this was the first one that landed in in this one. Um in ' 07, I should have really labeled this differently. Not since ' 06. I just said in '07, this is what we did. Um we made some sweeping changes to to um normalize and say that this this sort of applies to all fora. uh and and we we specifically tried to use the the term or define the term fora forum to uh mean all public IATF participation channels um and there was some um changes related to obsoleting RFC 3934 um and I don't know what explicit admin responsibility means sorry but you can look at the pull request and that will make it clear what was meant um and finally so Pete Resnik uh made a proposal on the working group list that uh we found compelling and I guess you did too as participants and and we rolled that in and we words smmithed a little bit and that is sort of the the final approach to how the moderator team and chairs intersect and yesterday when I asked the chairs whether it would be helpful to get a a draft submitted uh for discussion here in '08 I did an editorial pass and I fixed a bunch of wording things none of which I consider to be content related. is like terminology moving some sentences around adding like lines spaces and stuff like that. Um and with that I think we're done. So, so the main changes just to sort of uh wrap it up is basically um a shift in uh talking about the moderator team and what it does to talking about the moderation policy for the ITF of which this team is a part but also the chairs are an integral part and more clearly clearly defining specifically what the role of the team the chairs the area directors u uh and the ISG is And I think the the summary is that we landed on that the chairs are really tasked to continue to moderate their working groups and all the public participation channels that the working group uses. Um the moderator team may um also moderate if they believe the chairs are um awol i.e. they're not not doing anything visible. In order to prevent the situation from happening where the chairs have done something and the moderator team is unaware, we task the chairs with informing the moderator team when they are u um doing moderation actions which might for example Stephen's example from the list yesterday include sending an offlist uh nudge to say hey maybe cool it a little bit. It would be helpful for the moderator team to know that that happened so that they don't feel the need to step in and say chairs didn't do anything. Let's do something. Um, but I think at least sort of I haven't looked at the list this morning. I think some of us at least landed on maybe we don't need to overdefine uh handling of these edge cases and instead uh rely on everybody here trying to do the right thing and there being an appeals process for when things really should go wrong which we all hope they don't. And I think that's my last slide. Yes, it is. Questions? Ted? So the way you just described the process and what the document actually says don't seem to be the same to me. >> Okay. What the document says uh is correct. What I said is then wrong. What how how so how do I misspoke? I'm sorry. >> Uh yeah, no worries. Um, so the the thing that the thing that I'm slightly uncomfortable with in the document is that it the document when to my reading and I'm not saying my reading is correct, but to my reading the document says that the moderators must >> um attempt to contact the working group chairs and wait for some indeterminate period of time before the working group chairs respond, which is not what you said. >> Correct. >> What you Okay. >> So I I I simplified it and maybe I oversimplified it. So yes, so the moderator team before they take action when they believe that the chairs haven't done something should reconfirm with the chair. So the chairs should inform the moderator team and the moderator team if that information hasn't happened or the chairs haven't done anything they should reconfirm with the chairs first what is the state here and if only if they don't hear from the chairs and they haven't seen anything public should they after some time that it's TBD moderate >> right and I think you know just my personal opinion on this the time is usually probably not that urgent but sometimes it is >> and I think that is not currently captured in the text and I don't know how to capture that but I feel like right now if I were to read that text as a moderator and think about what I'm allowed to do I would assume that I'm not allowed to intervene immediately in situation even in situations where that would be required. >> Okay. So I I I think that would be the wrong reading and and maybe we can change words to make it clear because I think there's the moderator team I think should feel empowered in cases where they believe it's urgent that something be done and they can't either they don't have information from the chairs or the chairs are not responsive >> they should do something and then if there's a disagreement between the chairs and the moderator team about what happened we can unroll it. Yeah, I think we have that part. >> That part we have. Um, so maybe we if you can if you have a sentence or two that that you can do a PR forward, stick it in an email, that would be helpful. >> Okay, thanks. >> But I'm glad that's sort of the level of things that that get raised. U Pete goes to mic. >> Yeah. And the overall is that I'm happy with where we are. Um, and I think I have the same reaction to Ted's concern that I did to uh Stephen's concern on the list, which is these sort of fall into the edgy edge cases. I Steven's example was okay, the chair does something moderationwise, doesn't for whatever reason, accidental or otherwise, mention it to the mod team, and then goes on holiday. And and this starts to sound like an old George Carlin joke about, you know, I'm on a ship at sea and, you know, the the person goes into a coma and then we've crossed the international date line and okay, these things will happen. These are edge cases. Um, and I kind of feel the same way about TEDs, which is the number of times where the timing is so wildly important that the moderator sends a message to the chair and it doesn't come back in an hour or a half hour or whatever the the immediacy um uh requires and then makes the decision and what that timing should I'm not that worried about it. I I understand those cases do exist, but I think they're relatively rare when the intervention has to happen that quickly. So, I'm willing to leave it in the noise and uh if you know Ted or Steven can propose some text that covers edge cases better, peachy, but I'm I'm afraid we're going to start to dig ourselves a hole. So Ted Ted Lemon again um I don't want to put too fine a point on it but there are certain sorts of behaviors that when they occur must be stopped immediately. Um that would include uh let's say sexual harassment which we've seen on mailing lists that should be stopped immediately. There should be no waiting period. There should be no consultant with the chairs. That should be stopped immediately. This is the situation I'm talking about here. And it's it is yes it is an edge case. It doesn't happen very often. when it does happen, we should do the right thing immediately. That's my concern. >> Pete, again, I I'll I'll say that in a case of sexual harassment um or any kind of harassment, um the the levers that need to be pulled start to look a little different because that's the point where I would actually want the MOD team to say, "Whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa. We can call this out and say, "That looks like harassment. Cut it out now." But they should also be pulling the ombbuds team lever and and and get things rolling. That's a serious matter. And you know, I I think if that needs to be called out more specifically in this document, that's fine. But that's one where I also don't want either a chair or mod team to go it alone. They need to yelp and say, "Whoa, stop. That's bad." But then get start to get other people involved in that. uh whether it's uh you know IASG folks or the OMBbuds team or everybody >> or the ITF LLC >> or the ITF LLC because the police need to be called or whatever. I I mean >> yes, if we want to make that explicit that's fine. Um but um that's not a normal mod team edge case as far as I'm concerned. So, so do we believe that words need a change in a document to address any of this? I guess it's most mostly a question for Ted. You you nodding. Okay. If if you wouldn't mind sort of proposing an edit or enthusiastic thumbs up. Thank you. And and we can we can discuss it. Uh that would be helpful. Anything else there? No, it is Ted and Pete. Yeah, who have not taken themselves out of Q as one does in these cases. >> Well, the chair is correct. Anyways, um any other discussion about this document which I guess then brings me to passing it on to John and Lisa about the question whether this is ready to be last called or not. >> Yeah, I mean uh so we've heard from some people in the room here. We do have some people online. I don't know if anyone online has anything they want to add to the conversation thus far. You know, I think what I've seen on the list and Lisa obviously um feel free to chime in on this. You know, I I think we've seen this direction come to a set of um more narrow PRs that seem to have widespread support. Um obviously in a case like this, there's always always going to be people who are a bit in the rough. I I've seen people who have self-identified recognizing that they are going to be in the rough of the decision here. And so um my intuition at least would be that we are probably um we could say delta a PR that you are currently drafting Ted as we sit here. Um like delta that like I think we might be ready to go forward with that. Does anybody disagree with that uh online in the chat? Um >> do you want to do a poll? >> We could do a poll. You know these tools are so fancy I forget we even have these polls. Uh >> I don't know how many people we have online but in the room it's pretty small number. >> Yeah. >> Are we ready for working group last call on the ModPod processes document. A vote should be available. >> You can plus warn me on the yes because I'm logged in on my laptop but not on my phone so I can't click on the thing. But at the moment I'm voting zero because I'm not logged in on my phone. >> There's a 100% yes. Oh no. Oh, so close. I jinxed it. I jinxed it. >> 90% yes. All right. So, we may have four more no opinions here. Uh, going once, going twice. All right. So, I did not see there are any nos. So, um I think in so far as we have our coalition of the willing here that is uh willing to look at the overall situation. Yes, Chris, I'm sorry. >> Uh yeah. Um so I read through the draft this morning and yes in general I'm very happy with it. Um the the one thing I noted that was unclear which does we don't need to wait for the working group last call for but it's just the part where it says um all moderation actions that restrict posting rights shall be periodically reported to the ISG and immediately to those against who the actions take effect. what and and I read that I thought what's a rest what's an action that restricts posting rights so is putting someone in um it so in posttorious in putting them into hold for moderation state so any new emails that come in go into that hold state is that an action that restricts posting rights >> I mean I'd say things milder than that would qualify yeah >> yeah so so I' so we should elaborate that a little more I think but that's okay that's all I noted >> so so maybe something like anything that prevents messages from reaching the forum immediately or without review or something like that so anything that anything that restricts messages from directly being visible in the forum would be a posting okay do you feel like sending us a PR or an email suggestion with a wording Um, >> you can say no. >> Yes. No, I >> I don't immediately have an idea of what the wording, but I should Yeah, I could spend >> Okay. >> a couple of minutes thinking about what what I think would work there. I mean, I'm one thing I'm thinking is it would be helpful for the moderation team to Well, I don't know. It might be helpful if the moderation team has the discretion to place someone in hold for moderation without telling them immediately. I don't know whether that's that latitude is something we need to >> okay >> that that would have to be a discussion in the group I think. >> Yeah, >> there are some hard edges I can imagine on that. Yeah. >> So this is Pete. I I've been thinking about um and I've had a conversation in a different context outside of IETF about this about having in mind a distinction between um policy and procedures and the policy should be pretty broad and my gut is that the moderation team should be coming up with not just guidelines for you know what stays in what goes out um but also these kinds of questions of when they you know what kinds of actions they notify the ISG or they notify the person make that list the community is going to be looking at that list anyway and doing review of that list and can say whoa guys you need to do it even in these cases um and I think that's okay there's going to be community review this should set sort of the broad brush strokes and leave it to the mod team to come up with the really detailed stuff >> and So if Chris is comfortable with you all write down what you think the appropriate point is to let the person know what the constraints on that. Maybe there's a delay because we've thrown the person into moderation but for whatever reason we haven't told them immediately or we haven't told the ISG. I think that's okay for you all to write down the details of we've got the broad brush stroke in this document which is you got to tell the ISG at some point if you blocked posting. >> Yeah, I'd agree with that. Right. I mean and it's going to be a discussion and I think the ISG is tasked with creating a list for the discussion of the moderation procedures. Um in order to sort of uh nitpick that what Chris suggested. So, so I think if you have this sort of silent moderation bucket, it it is visible to persons that get thrown in there because their posts are going to be delayed for some lists while not others. So, so in effect, you're exposing that that has happened and so you might as well be transparent and tell them, look, we put you in this for this reason. Um, and and you know, we might take you out. >> That's it. >> Yeah. So, totally different topic. Um I I forgot to mention this earlier. Um in the appendix it feels like it could use a little bit more text to to just absolutely exclude lawyering um based on the appendix the the one that lists the the examples. Yeah. And I can send you some text you might or might not like. >> Yeah. I mean it you cannot be over stating this because people immediately go like for the for the ITF list, right? every single time people were like but it's not I didn't send like a CFP for or even I did send a CFP but ISOC is co-sponsoring it and doesn't this so this is really sort of yes but okay we're going to set a CPR from Ted on that thank you >> okay so if we are going to wait for a few last notes from Ted before we do last uh working group last call when when Ted what media imagine we could see such things So, I would suggest that pull requests are part of the working group last call process and you don't need to wait. >> Fair enough. Yeah, you're right. We could do that. >> And I'm going to be on vacation next week. So, unless Elliot hits buttons in in GitHub, which he might well do. >> Look at this. Elliot is now in the chat. Elliot, >> long awaited over. It's all over now, man. You You missed it. We like totally solved everything and >> Okay, I'll leave. >> But is there anything Elliot you want to say about all this? I don't know when you joined and how much of this you caught? Um >> um >> it seems like I just joined. So >> yeah. So we're we're we're entering we are imminently entering working group last call for the document is what you are walking into. So, I hope you're into something good. >> Uh, yeah, Laura, if there are any buttons you need pushed, just let me know and I'll push them. >> You're very soft. >> Yeah, you're a little We can't quite hear you. >> I said if there are any buttons to be pushed, I'll be happy to push them. >> Okay. >> Okay. So, I I would So, this is just because I'm out next week, maybe we can do like an extra week working group last call or like there's no rush, right? because it it gives me a little bit more time to finish wrapping up the editing and Elliot nodding as well. >> We could also just wait a week and then start working. Why don't we do that and then we can get these PRs in and like have this all looking so beautiful. There will be it will be an unimpeachable document by the time last call comes about. >> You can impeach documents. >> Yes. Yes. Yes. Well, I can always appeal them. >> Okay. I I as far as I know, we have no other business. Is there any other business before this August body or um are we on our way out, Lisa? >> Um I'm good. And uh just thanks to the editors and thanks to everybody who's uh providing these comments >> and thanks chairs. >> Thank you. All right. You know I believe um at 9:59 we can now conclude this meeting and if there are no other comments or very exciting look at this. Look at what we have done here. People think we can't do these kinds of things. We have done these things. Mic drop. Thank you for coming. >> Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. We did. All right. Thank you. We're done. Bye Lisa. Get some sleep. >> Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. That doesn't requ Jesus Christ.
